source: CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
title: Ensure credentials unused for 45 days or more are disabled

input_row.source: CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure credentials unused for 45 days or more are disabled
input_row.description: AWS IAM users can access AWS resources using different types of credentials, such as passwords or access keys. It is recommended that all credentials that have been unused for 45 days or more be deactivated or removed.
input_row.rationale: Disabling or removing unnecessary credentials will reduce the window of opportunity for credentials associated with a compromised or abandoned account to be used.
input_row.audit: Perform the following to determine if unused credentials exist: From Console: 1. Login to the AWS Management Console 2. Click Services 3. Click IAM 4. Click on Users 5. Click the Settings (gear) icon. 6. Select Console last sign-in, Access key last used, and Access Key Id 7. Click on Close 8. Check and ensure that Console last sign-in is less than 45 days ago. Note - Never means the user has never logged in. 9. Check and ensure that Access key age is less than 45 days and that Access key last used does not say None If the user hasn't signed into the Console in the last 45 days or Access keys are over 45 days old refer to the remediation. From Command Line: Download Credential Report: 1. Run the following commands: aws iam generate-credential-report aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d | cut -d, -f1,4,5,6,9,10,11,14,15,16 | grep -v '^<root_account>' Ensure unused credentials do not exist: 2. For each user having password_enabled set to TRUE , ensure password_last_used_date is less than 45 days ago. • When password_enabled is set to TRUE and password_last_used is set to No_Information , ensure password_last_changed is less than 45 days ago. 3. For each user having an access_key_1_active or access_key_2_active to TRUE , ensure the corresponding access_key_n_last_used_date is less than 45 days ago. • When a user having an access_key_x_active (where x is 1 or 2) to TRUE and corresponding access_key_x_last_used_date is set to N/A, ensure access_key_x_last_rotated is less than 45 days ago.
input_row.remediation: From Console: Perform the following to manage Unused Password (IAM user console access) 1. Login to the AWS Management Console: 2. Click Services 3. Click IAM 4. Click on Users 5. Click on Security Credentials 6. Select user whose Console last sign-in is greater than 45 days 7. Click Security credentials 8. In section Sign-in credentials, Console password click Manage 9. Under Console Access select Disable 10. Click Apply Perform the following to deactivate Access Keys: 1. Login to the AWS Management Console: 2. Click Services 3. Click IAM 4. Click on Users 5. Click on Security Credentials 6. Select any access keys that are over 45 days old and that have been used and • Click on Make Inactive 7. Select any access keys that are over 45 days old and that have not been used and • Click the X to Delete References: 1. CCE-78900-8 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/best-practices.html#remove- credentials 3. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_finding- unused.html 4. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_passwords_a dmin-change-user.html 5. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_access- keys.html Additional Information: <root_account> is excluded in the audit since the root account should not be used for day-to-day business and would likely be unused for more than 45 days.
input_row.references: 1. CCE-78900-8 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/best-practices.html#remove- credentials 3. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_finding- unused.html 4. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_passwords_a dmin-change-user.html 5. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_access- keys.html Additional Information: <root_account> is excluded in the audit since the root account should not be used for day-to-day business and would likely be unused for more than 45 days.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

- The control described is primarily concerned with IAM users and their credential usage, which is a standard AWS service feature accessible through AWS APIs.
- The audit field describes a process that can be directly translated into AWS CLI commands to generate and retrieve credential reports.
- The use of AWS IAM's `generate-credential-report` and `get-credential-report` commands for auditing aligns with available boto3 methods, allowing for programmatic checking.
- The compliance check is based on the attributes and metrics that can be directly retrieved and analyzed via AWS APIs, such as `password_last_used_date` and `access_key_n_last_used_date`.
- IAM credential usage data is readily available through AWS API, making it feasible to automate the checks for unused credentials.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_iam_user_credentials_unused_for_45_days_disabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

- Utilize the boto3 IAM client to perform API calls, specifically `generate_credential_report()` to generate a report and `get_credential_report()` to fetch the report content.
- Parse the credential report to check for IAM users with:
  - Passwords enabled and `password_last_used` older than 45 days.
  - Access keys with `access_key_1_last_used` or `access_key_2_last_used` dated more than 45 days ago or undefined.
- Ensure that active credentials (passwords and access keys) are not unused for over 45 days.
- Confirm compliance by checking:
  - For each user with `password_enabled` set to TRUE, ensure `password_last_used_date` is within 45 days.
  - For access keys, verify that `access_key_1_last_used_date` and `access_key_2_last_used_date` fall within the last 45 days or, if not used, were rotated within that period.
- Flag any non-compliant credentials for remediation actions, specifically disabling or deleting them if they are unused as per AWS security best practices.
- Reporting should categorize findings by user and the age of last used credentials, providing remediation guidance as per AWS documentation.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. AWS provides the necessary APIs and CLI commands to automate the process of generating and analyzing IAM credential reports.
- The use of `generate-credential-report` and `get-credential-report` commands is appropriate for this control, as they allow for programmatic access to IAM credential usage data.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9
- The automation details are technically accurate. The use of `generate_credential_report()` and `get_credential_report()` methods in boto3 is correct and aligns with AWS best practices for retrieving IAM credential data.
- The logic for checking `password_last_used` and `access_key_n_last_used` is sound. However, the explanation could be slightly more detailed regarding handling undefined last used dates, which typically indicate that the credentials have never been used.
- The program name `aws_iam_user_credentials_unused_for_45_days_disabled` is descriptive and follows a logical naming convention, but could be slightly refined for clarity.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- The explanation should explicitly mention handling cases where `password_last_used` or `access_key_n_last_used` is `None`, as this indicates the credentials have never been used and should be flagged for immediate action.
- Consider adding a step to log the actions taken for auditing purposes, such as disabling or deleting credentials, which can be useful for compliance tracking.
- The program name could be improved for clarity: `aws_iam_user_credentials_check_unused_for_45_days`. This name clearly indicates that the program is checking for unused credentials over a specified period.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- The assessment is well-aligned with AWS capabilities and security best practices. The use of AWS APIs and CLI commands is appropriate and technically sound, giving high confidence in the assessment's validity.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Keep as-is with minor modifications for clarity in the program name and handling of undefined last used dates.
- Ensure that the explanation includes logging actions for compliance tracking and explicitly addresses credentials that have never been used.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identify that AWS exposes all necessary IAM credential usage data via the `generate_credential_report` and `get_credential_report` APIs, allowing for full automation of this control. The audit and remediation steps described in the control can be programmatically executed by parsing the credential report and checking for unused credentials. No manual inspection is required, as all relevant properties are available via AWS APIs.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
aws_iam_user_credentials_unused_for_45_days_disabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- AWS Resource/Service: IAM Users and their credentials (passwords and access keys)
- API/CLI Method: 
  - boto3: `iam.generate_credential_report()`, `iam.get_credential_report()`
  - AWS CLI: `aws iam generate-credential-report`, `aws iam get-credential-report`
- Properties to Check:
  - `password_last_used`
  - `access_key_1_last_used`
  - `access_key_2_last_used`
  - `access_key_1_active`
  - `access_key_2_active`
- Pass Criteria:
  - All IAM users have either:
    - Console password disabled, or `password_last_used` is within the last 45 days.
    - All active access keys have `access_key_n_last_used` within the last 45 days.
    - If `password_last_used` or `access_key_n_last_used` is "N/A" or blank (never used), those credentials are disabled or removed.
- Fail Criteria:
  - Any IAM user has an enabled console password with `password_last_used` older than 45 days or never used.
  - Any active access key has `access_key_n_last_used` older than 45 days or never used.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
There were no substantive disagreements; both AI opinions agreed on the automated approach. The second opinion suggested minor improvements (explicit handling of "never used" credentials), which are incorporated in the automation details above.

**SOURCE:** CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
